coredata.dc:title: Selective visual detection of histamine and ascorbic acid through the rapid gel-sol transition of luminescent alginate hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Barik, Balaram
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent hydrogel has been synthesized by crosslinking sodium alginate with Ni2+ in the presence of PEI-derived carbon dots. Such a hydrogel shows a quick selective visual response towards histamine through rapid gel-to-sol transition followed by the release of CD into the sol solution. The amount of histamine can be quantified by measuring the fluorescence intensity of sol solution at an excitation wavelength3 360 nm. The sensor's detection limit in the turn-on intensity mode goes up to 0.63 nM. Utilizing the detoxification reaction of histamine by ascorbic acid, the present sensing platform has been explored to detect ascorbic acid. Due to low-cost and easy synthesis, such hydrogel-based fluorescent chemosensors are potentially suitable for monitoring histamine in serum and fish samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Gel-to-sol
Carbon dot
Histamine
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of crosslinking in Ni2+-alginate hydrogel
Response of CDs@Ni/Alg gel towards histamine
Effect of anion on stability of the CDs@Ni/Alg

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors acknowledge SERB , DST, Grant no. CRG/2018/001717 for financial support for this work. BB thanks NIT Rourkela for providing Ph.D. fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectrum of PEI-CD, (b) excitation-dependent emission spectra of PEI-CD, (c) Excitation dependent emission. Dots show the excitation wavelength.

(a) PL response of CDs@Ni/Alg gel towards histamine, (b) Visual representation of histamine sensing by CDs@Ni/Alg, (c) Stern-volmer plot, (d) Response time of the sensor towards histamine and ascorbic acid.

(a) PL response of histamine containing gel towards L-Ascorbate (b) Linear Stern volmer plot showing the limit of detection (c)Visual representation of L-Ascorbate sensing (d) Selectivity of L-Ascorbate in presence of other biomolecules.
Hydrothermal synthesis of PEI capped N, S doped CD.
Mechanistic pathway of the Histamine and L-ascorbate sensing.
Recoveries of histamine from real samples.

head.ce:title: Selective visual detection of histamine and ascorbic acid through the rapid gel-sol transition of luminescent alginate hydrogel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, 769008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, India-769008

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent hydrogel has been synthesized by crosslinking sodium alginate with Ni2+ in the presence of PEI-derived carbon dots. Such a hydrogel shows a quick selective visual response towards histamine through rapid gel-to-sol transition followed by the release of CD into the sol solution. The amount of histamine can be quantified by measuring the fluorescence intensity of sol solution at an excitation wavelength3 360 nm. The sensor's detection limit in the turn-on intensity mode goes up to 0.63 nM. Utilizing the detoxification reaction of histamine by ascorbic acid, the present sensing platform has been explored to detect ascorbic acid. Due to low-cost and easy synthesis, such hydrogel-based fluorescent chemosensors are potentially suitable for monitoring histamine in serum and fish samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine(HA), 4-(2-aminoethyl)-imidazole is an important aminogenic neurotransmitter that is produced by decarboxylation of histidine in mast cells and basophils [1,2]. The normal level of histamine in human blood is 213.5 ± 178.9 nmol/L and urine is 4.01 ± 3.4 nmol/L [3]. Abnormal histamine level leads to different types of diseases like atopic dermatitis, Alzheimer’s disease, acute allergy, and various neuropsychiatric disorders [4,5]. Therefore, accurate determination of histamine concentration in biological samples like human blood and urine is generally prescribed as an early diagnostic test for the timely detection of neurological disorders. In addition, histamine is produced by enzymatic decarboxylation of histidine by microorganisms in fish. Thus, histamine accumulation can be considered as an indicator for the storage, handling, and spoilage of fish [6]. Numerous techniques such as electrochemical sensing [7], gas chromatography (GC), capillary zone electrophoresis [8], High-Pressure Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [9], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [10], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4] have been explored for the assay of histamine in biological and food samples. Even though these instrument-based techniques have the advantages of high accuracy and reproducibility, they have a few inalienable limitations such as high cost, tedious operation, and complicated sampling procedure [10]. Alternatively, blessed with several merits such as high detection sensitivity, easy operation, and use of portable device fluorescent detection methods [11,12] may be suitable for on-site detection of histamine [13–15]. In this connection, few materials such as organic molecular probes [16], metal complex [17], histamine blue doped mesoporous silica [18], gold upconversion nanoparticles [4], inorganic-organic hybrid nanoparticles [13], gold nanorods [19], have been explored for the detection of histamine spectrophotometrically. Although these sensors have accomplished selective and satisfactory sensitivity towards histamine, most of these methods employ expensive analytical instruments directly in the field. Again, an assignable time delay which causes unintended changes in the sample, which ultimately produces erroneous data. Consequently, developing an easy and technically sound method for histamine sensing is essential.

Apart from conventional intensity-based measurements the recognition signals can be acquired by optical sensors through visual observation of color, deformation or more sophisticatedly by light reflection, luminescence lifetime, and so on. Hydrogel has been emerging as an attractive material for environmental monitoring and point-of-care-testing thanks to its aqueous environments which could host a wide variety of analytes in their natural forms and functions [20]. Further, flexible and versatile photonic gels fabricated by incorporating conventional photonic structures in the hydrogel offer new possibilities in chemical and biological sensors. In such hydrogel sensors, the phase transition process is stimulated by analytical targets that produce a visual optical signal, providing a clear-cut and straightforward detection of the analytes without applying sophisticated instruments [21]. Among polymeric hydrogels, alginate is a biocompatible polymer widely used in the pharmaceutical and food industries. Divalent metal ions readily induce the cross-linking in alginate to form hydrogels [22,23]. External hydrophilic solutions are easily trapped inside the alginate hydrogel network due to its porous nature and hydrophilicity. Furthermore, the storing of colorimetric probes and enzymes is facilitated inside the water-enriched alginate hydrogel without any impairment. All these merits make alginate an excellent candidate for the construction of biosensors.

On the other hand, carbon dots are emerging nano lights because of their easy synthesis, solubility and excellent luminescence properties. The unique tunable luminescence properties of carbon dots have been extensively explored in detection of cations [24], anions [25] and a wide range of biomolecules [26]. Nevertheless, bare CDs tend to aggregate under neutral conditions, leading to a dramatic fall in fluorescence intensity [27]. So encapsulation of CD in a supramolecular network has become a popular strategy to stabilize CDs and improve their performance [28,29]. In particular, tremendous interest has been driven in the exploration of such CD-incorporated photonic gels in the detection of various sensing targets such as pH [30], metal ions [31], bacteria [32], alkaline phosphates [33], and antibiotics [34]. However, despite significant research progress, such photogenic gels have not been explored to detect vital biogenic amine such as histamine.

In this paper for the first time, we have developed luminescent alginate hydrogel for naked-eye detection of histamine. Ni2+ is used as a crosslinker and blue fluorescent carbon dot as the optical component to synthesize this photonic gel. The positively charged carbon dots have been incorporated in the alginate gel to form a highly cross-linked Ni/alginate/carbon dot fluorescent hydrogel. As histamine has a high affinity towards Ni2+ [35], the addition of traces of histamine solution can disrupt cross-linking due to competitive binding of Ni2+ with histamine leading to collapse of gel structure. Such histamine-responsive gel-to-sol transition releases fluorescent carbon dots into solution, and hence the intensity of sol increases, thus achieving a sensible detection towards histamine. As L-ascorbic acid decomposes histamine in the presence of Cu2+, by applying L-ascorbic acid/Cu2+ detoxification reaction mixture of histamine, a visual detection platform for vitamin C has also been established.

Citric acid(CA), sodium phosphate monobasic(NaH2PO4), Sodium alginate, Poly ethyleneimine (branched), and glucosamine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Thio urea(TU), glutathione, L-ascorbic acid, and Nickel chloride(NiCl2) was purchased from Merck. L-alanine, L-cysteine hydrochloride, and L-histidine were purchased from Spectro chem EDTA, Sodium carbonate (NaCO3) and Sodium sulfate (NaSO4) was procured from Fisher scientific.

CA (42 mg), TU(84 mg), and PEI (3 ml, 10%) were taken in 14 ml of Millipore water in a glass beaker. After 30 min of stirring the transparent solution was transferred to the autoclave in a teflon container. The reaction proceeded at 180º C for 20 h. The color of the mixture was turned yellow, indicating the formation of carbon dots ( Scheme 1).

900 μL of NiCl2 aqueous solution was added to 2 ml of alginate aqueous solution (wt%, 6). Then it was stirred at room temp 400 rpm for 1 h. Afterwards, the unreacted reagent was removed by washing hydrogel with ultrapure water for three time.

CDs@Alg/Ni was prepared by adding 100 μL CDs in 2 ml of aqueous alginate solution (wt% 6) over 30 min 900 μL of NiCl2 aqueous solution was added to initiate the gelation of alginate. Then the reaction was continued for 1 h. After that, insoluble hydrogel was washed with water for three times to remove unreacted reagent.

1 ml of CDs@Alg/Ni hydrogel was kept in a 2 ml eppendorf then 500 μL of different concentrations of histamine was added in each eppendorf. After addition, it was kept in incubation chamber at 37 °C for two hours. Further, 10 μL of the supernatant was collected from each eppendorf and added in 3 ml of PBS buffer (0.05 M, pH 7) for fluorescence measurement using a spectrofluorometric (Horiba-PTI, QM-400) at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

The optical properties of carbon dots were investigated using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The as-prepared CDs have two absorption bands at 233 and 360 nm. The peak at 233 nm corresponds to a π → π* transition involving aromatic sp2 carbons and the peak at 360 nm can be assigned to the intrinsic absorption of n → π* transition of the CO bond in carbon cores ( Fig. 1a) [36]. Moreover, Fig. 1b shows that emission peak of purified CD varied from 430 nm to 630 nm with the gradual change of excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 580 nm. The emission became stronger and red-shifted when, the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 380 nm. When excited from 380 to 580 nm, the emission peak kept red shifting with a remarkable decrease in intensity (Fig. 1c). The excitation-dependent optical properties were identical to that of CDs reported in literature [37]. The microstructure of PEI-CDs was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images (Fig. S1a) showed that the size of PEI-CDs is distributed in the range from 2.5 to 6 nm, with a mean average size of 3.5 nm. However, larger sized C nanoparticles or aggregation were not observed for using PEI as a surface passivating agent. The crystal structure of PEI-CDs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S1b). The presence of a broad peak centered at X = 29.34 (d=0.3 nm) showed the amorphous nature of phase structure, which is identical to the characteristic [002] plane of graphene. In the Raman Spectrum of PEI-CD (Fig. S1c), the peak at 1375 cm−1 is associated with graphitic (nanocrystalline) carbon while the peak at 1560 cm−1 corresponded to dangling (amorphous) carbon material. The ratio of intensities Id/Ig is 0.98 which indicates more graphitic nature of PEI-CD. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S1d), two broad peaks at 3460 cm−1, 3158 cm−1 correspond to the existence of –OH and N-H stretching vibrations respectively. The peaks at 1365, 1560, 1647 cm−1 are ascribed to C-N, N-H, CO respectively, which confirmed the surface of CD is chemically capped with PEI molecules. The capped PEI molecules provide surface passivation along with solubility and stability to CDs in water. It is well observed that PEI-CD is positively charged over wide pH range due to protonation reactions of different pendant amine groups. The surface charge of PEI-CD was positive up to pH 7, after that the value became negative at higher pH (Fig. S2) because of the dissociation of amide and deprotonation of carboxylic groups, which could cause the reduction in fluorescence intensity [38]. The emission spectrum of PEI-capped CD largely varies with pH, implying that the as prepared CDs were very sensitive to acidic as well as basic solvents (Fig. S3a). The stability of resultant PEI-CDs was investigated by observing fluorescence intensity over 3 h (Fig. S3b) at pH 7. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was slightly decreased and preserved the initial intensity after 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of crosslinking in Ni2+-alginate hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the crosslinking ability of Ni2+ on alginate, different concentration of Ni2+ were taken for crosslinking. Ni2+ can successfully crosslink alginate to form Ni/Alg gel like other divalent ions. The gel size also depends upon the concentration of Ni2+ (Fig. S4). By taking this advantage, a florescent alginate hydrogel was further designed by incorporating CDs in the Ni/Alg gel, during gelation. When Ni2 + was added to the CD/Alg mixture, sol-gel transition happens instantaneously by forming CDs@Ni/Alg gel (Fig. S5). The green fluorescence of insoluble hydrogel confirmed that the CDs were successfully incorporated in the gel matrix. The volume of CDs@Ni/Alg also depends upon the concentration of Ni2+ as before happened in Ni/Alg which evidenced that the gel is free from adsorbed CDs. However, the zeta potential of CD is + 2.5 and there is every possibility for adsorption of CD on the negatively charged alginate gel by electrostatic attraction. As a result, very less fluorescence was noted from the supernatant after immerging CDs@Ni/Alg gel in water for 12 h (Fig. S6). Before testing the responsive ability of CDs@Ni/Alg towards histamine, the fluorescence response of free CD towards Ni2+ as well as histamine were investigated (Fig. S7). It was confirmed that Ni2+ and histamine have no impact on the fluorescence of PEI derived CD at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CDs@Ni/Alg gel towards histamine
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the responsiveness of CDs@Ni/Alg hydrogel towards histamine, the structural stability of fluorescent CDs@Ni/Alg hydrogel was observed in the presence of different concentrations of histamine. It was found that in absence of histamine, the CDs@Ni/Alg maintains stable three-dimensional structure, and no fluorescence was noticed from the solution part. However, when histamine is added, CDs@Ni/Alg was disintegrated, and intense fluorescence (λEx 360, λem 452 nm) was measured from its sol solution ( Fig. 2a). Furthermore, neither Ni2+ nor histamine could influence the fluorescence of CDs which is confirmed by the almost unchanged fluorescence of free CDs in the presence of individual Ni2+ or histamine (Fig. S7). From Fig. 2b, we can see that the gel size of CDs@Ni/Alg reduced gradually and at last completely transformed with the increase of the histamine concentration, which is associated with the rise in CD fluorescence (452 nm) in the sol solution. The kinetics of CD release into the sol solution was monitored by recording fluorescence intensity of sol at λem 452 nm (Fig. 2c). In the first 10 min, there was a rapid increase in the FL intensity of sol, which reached a plateau after 25 mins (Fig. 2d). The digital photograph of gel size at different time intervals evidenced that it takes 10 mins to initiate the reaction and after 25 mins the entire gel is converted to sol (Fig. S8). These results noticeably validate that a Ni/Alg gel is a smart stimuli responsive hydrogel that responds to histamine. CDs can be used as a visual indicator to observe the gel-to–sol transition. The regular enhancement of fluorescence intensity with respect to concentration of the sol also indicates that this probe can be suitable for quantifying histamine. The linear relationship was reflected from the R2 value which is 0.994 (Fig. 2c). The detection limit for histamine was found to be 0.63 nM, which is comparable and even superior to the previously reported technique for detecting histamine (Table S1). Furthermore, as compared to other methods, this is the only method for naked-eye detection of histamine (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of anion on stability of the CDs@Ni/Alg
ce:section.ce:para: The response of CDs@Ni/Alg towards various anions such as EDTA, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, NO3 -, Cl- was examined to verify the stability of the gel in the presence of different anions (Fig. S9). It was observed that other anions could not disrupt gelation under similar sensing conditions except EDTA. Evidently, the Ni/Alg gel is highly specific towards histamine and EDTA should be ignored in Ni/Alg to histamine assay. Using high concentration of Ni2+ the stability of CDs@Ni/Alg hydrogel increases but the detection possibility decreases because higher concentration of histamine and more reaction time are needed to disrupt the hydrogel. Hence, a suitable concentration of Ni2+ has to be obtained to form a stable hydrogel that can be sensitive to histamine in stipulated reaction conditions. For this purpose, CDs@Ni/Alg hydrogel was prepared by using three different concentrations of Ni2+ (25, 28 and 30 mM) along with 6 wt% of alginate and the interaction resulting gels was investigated with a fixed histamine concentration (200 nM) (Fig. S10). It was observed that the rate of dissociation of the hydrogel enhanced with lowering the concentration of Ni2+. It took 2 h for complete dissociation of 25 mM nickel-containing alginate gel, however with 28, 30 mM nickel-containing hydrogel, it took 4 h and 10 h for complete disintegration, respectively. Hence 25 mM nickel is the optimized concentration for constructing CDs@Ni/Alg photonic gel sensing platform. The calibration curve was plotted with the histamine concentration ranging from 1 to 100 nM.

The interaction of CDs@Ni/Alg gel was tested with similar biomolecules such as histidine, glucosamine, glutathione, alanine, cysteine, and anions such as EDTA H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, NO3-, Cl- (Fig. S11a). It was observed that under similar sensing condition other anions or biomolecules could not disrupt gelation except EDTA. Evidently, the Ni/Alg gel is highly specific towards histamine and EDTA should be ignored in Ni/Alg in histamine assay. All these results reveal that the designed method is exceptionally selective for histamine. The interference test was carried out to examine whether the response of CDs@Ni/Alg gel towards histamine is affected by the presence of other anomalous biomolecule and cations along with histamine (Fig. S11b). Under similar sensing conditions, other biomolecules or ions could not influence the gel-sol transition of fluorescent CDs@Ni/Alg gel and hence no substantial change in the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant. Seemingly, the response of the CDs@Ni/Alg gel to histamine is precise, which is attributed to the higher affinity of Ni2+ to histamine than other anions and biomolecules.

When histamine was added to the gel solution, the network structure of CDs@Ni/Alg was disrupted ( Scheme 2). Because of the higher binding affinity of Ni2+ towards histamine, the formation of octahedral Ni2+-histamine coordination complex is triggered [35]. As a result, the CDs come out from the matrix into the sol solution and the phase transition of gel-sol was seen by naked eye. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of sol was enhanced with increase of histamine. In order to justify our proposed mechanism, we investigated the effect of histamine degradation on gel-sol transition of CDs@Ni/Alg fluorescent gel. It is well known that L-ascorbate generate H2O2 in the presence of Cu2+ which can break down histamine [39,40]. Hence, CDs@Ni/Alg have been used as a sensing platform for ascorbic acid. The emission intensity of the sol solution of CDs@Ni/Alg was recorded after incubating histamine solution with L-ascorbate of various concentrations in presence of Cu2+. As L-ascorbic acid was gradually added to the CD@Ni/Alg gel along with histamine, the fluorescence intensity of the sol solution was decreased which indicates improvement in restriction efficiency of gel-sol transition of CDs@Ni/Alg sensor induced by histamine. The sequential decrease in fluorescence intensity of sol with respect to ascorbic acid concentration indicates that CD@Ni/Alg can be used for the assay of L-ascorbate ( Fig. 3a). The sensor's response time towards ascorbic acid was found to be 10 mins (Fig. 2b).

The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 452 nm and ascorbic acid concentration ranging from 0.2 to 1 mM can be obtained with a correlation coefficient 0.99 as calculated from the calibration curve (Fig. 3b, c). The detection limit toward ascorbic acid was found to be 5 µM. Even this hydrogel-based sensing platform is selective towards ascorbic acid compared to other similar biomolecules like glutathione, glucose, cysteine, arginine, etc. in the above-mentioned concentration range (Fig. 3d).

To validate the applicability of CD@Ni/Alg in real blood serum and urine samples, histamine was spiked into serum with different concentrations. The concentration from the calibration plot revealed that the recovery percent of histamine in the spiked serum were in the range of 90–110% ( Table 1). Similarly, human urine samples were diluted twenty times with PBS buffer solution and then spiked with standard histamine. The recovery percentage was found to be 92–99.6% (Table 1). Similarly, in case of tuna fish samples (SI), the recovery percentage of the spiked sample was in the range of 93–98%. Recovery percentage very close to 100% indicates the feasibility of the developed sensor in practical applications.

In summary, histamine responsive highly fluorescent alginate hydrogel has been constructed by incorporating blue fluorescent carbon dot in alginate using Ni2+ as a crosslinker. The response of Ni/Alg gel to histamine can be monitored visually by gel-sol transition which implicates the release of CD into the solution. The detection limit of histamine comes down to 0.63 nM. The application of CD@Ni/Alg sensor has been validated in real blood serum, urine and fish samples. The straightforward working principle as well as selective response of CD@Ni/Alg towards histamine makes this alginate based fluorescence based sensor suitable for assay of histamine in real samples.

Balaram Barik: is responsible for all experimental work, data analysis and Writing – original draft of the manuscript. Sasmita Mohapatra: is responsible for Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing and correction of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge SERB, DST, Grant no. CRG/2018/001717 for financial support for this work. BB thanks NIT Rourkela for providing Ph.D. fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132128.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

J. Agric. Food Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent sensing probe for the sensitive detection of histamine based on molecular imprinting ionic liquid-modified quantum dots
Food Anal. Methods

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sasmita Mohapatra received her Bachelor degree (B.Sc.) with honors in Chemistry from Ravenshaw College, Odisha, India in 1999. She did her Masters in Chemistry from Utkal University, India in 2001. She then obtained her Ph. D. degree from Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur in 2007. Her area of doctoral research was synthesis and application of oxide nanomaterials. Currently she is working as an Associate Professor in National Institute of Technology, Rourkela. Her current research interest includes design of smart nanotheranostics for cancer therapy, fabrication and applications of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, fluorescence nanosensor for environmental applications.

Balaram Barik obtained his B.Sc. degree from Utkal University, Odisha in 2012, and M.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Sambalpur University in 2014. He is currently continuing his doctoral research in National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, Odisha. His research interest covers fabrication of fluorescence carbon nanomaterials for sensing and medical applications.
